THE STRUGGLE OF A FEMINIST

Six remakes since the original nearly a hundred years ago, and this one is different....how? 
Jo: Free-spirit, a writer, hot-tempered, non-conformist, longs for love
Meg: Lovely, demure, studious, dutiful, honorable
Beth: Sweet, peace-maker, quiet, beloved, everyone's heart
Amy: Spoiled, jealous, headstrong, reckless, an artist, follows her heart
Marmee: Feminist, unofficial head of household, protector of her girls
Laurie: Smitten with Jo, confidant with Meg, mourns the loss of Beth, marries Amy

Jo writes stories. Arranges plays for her and her sisters to act out. Is close to her Aunt March. Cuts off her heair and sells it to buy a ticket home for their father who is off fighting. Pursued by Laurie. Meg is happy to be a housewife (much to Jo's chagrin). Beth contracts scarlet fever. Amy causes trouble. Burns Jo's manuscript. Fight ensues. Amy nearly dies. Everyone kisses and makes up. Meg gets married. Jo rebuffs Laurie; sells her manuscript. Becomes a teacher. Amy goes to Paris and is praised for her paintings. Marries Laurie. Jo gets married.

Tell me how anything has changed in 80 years--other than the actresses? Yeah, I think I'll pass.

Written and directed by Greta Gerwig (who also directed ‘Lady Bird’), this is absolutely the WORST movie I’ve seen in recent years. It unequivocally affirms my worst opinion of Rotten Tomatoes, which positively rates this movie at 95%.

This movie is all-round bad. Its poorly written script fundamentally changes the original story just to inject a ‘faux’ feminist overtone. So is its casting. In trying to distinguish it from previous movie adaptations (and presupposing its audience already knows the story), this movie randomly flips the timeline and sequence of events back-and-forth. Not only does it kill plot cohesion, it fails to give most actors a chance to develop their roles. Purporting to address the issue of feminism of this era (Massachusetts during the Civil War in 1860’s), it blasts marriage as merely an ‘economic proposition’, which is true - except it does so casually in 3 lines of one dialog. 

In my view, the 1949 (Elizabeth Taylor) and 1994 (Susan Sarandon) adaptations of ‘Little Women’ are fantastic classics. Why they bother to make such an inferior one in 2019 really beats me. Only guess is : director Greta Gerwig has no clothes !

SPOILER FOR THOSE WHO HAVEN’T READ THE NOVEL

The line that Jo (Saoirse) says about how she’s “so sick of people saying that love is all a woman is fit for” and that whole monologue wasn’t in the novel. However, those were actually Louisa May Alcott’s words (the author of ‘Little Women’). She said it in response to her fans who kept asking why she finished the novel with Jo ending up with Friedrich instead of Teddy and how Jo choosing to keep her independence would still have been a better ending (I’ve seen this adaptation and it addresses and answers why Alcott had to end it that way.) Greta Gerwig chose to add it in the script as ‘Little Women’ is partly auto-biographical and based Jo off herself.
